assignments.name: Case Study
assignments.intro: Read through the case study, and submit your response as an assignment.
assignments.activity:
Case Study

In this case study, explain the different programming paradigms can be used in developing a system for the same:

An online library management system is designed to manage the borrowing and returning of books in a library. This system needs to handle multiple types of users (e.g., students, teachers, librarians) and maintain a catalog of books. The system should be scalable, easy to maintain, and modular to allow for future enhancements.

assignments.activity: This case study demonstrates how encapsulation can be applied in Java programming to manage the inventory of a library system while ensuring data integrity and controlled access to book information. OUK university library wants to develop a Library Management System (LMS) to efficiently manage its book inventory and facilitate borrowing and returning books for students and faculty members. The system should ensure the security and integrity of the library's data. Requirements: The system should have a class Book to represent a book with the following attributes: title (String): Title of the book. author (String): Author of the book. isbn (String): International Standard Book Number. availableCopies (int): Number of copies available for borrowing. Implement encapsulation by making all attributes of the Book class private. Provide getter and setter methods for each attribute to access and modify the book details. Ensure that the number of available copies cannot be negative by validating inputs in the setter method. Write a Library class to manage the library's book inventory, including methods to add new books, borrow books, return books, and display book details. Use encapsulation to protect the integrity of the book inventory and ensure controlled access to book information.

assignments.activity: ABC Company wants to develop an Employee Management System (EMS) to manage its employee records. The system should allow adding new employees, updating their details, and retrieving employee information. Implement the system to have a class Employee to represent an employee with the following attributes: id (int): Unique identifier for each employee. name (String): Name of the employee. department (String): Department in which the employee works. salary (double): Salary of the employee. The Employee class should provide encapsulation by making all attributes private.

assignments.name: MASTERY  EXERCISE
assignments.intro: After going through the module presentation above, use the virtual lab tool (CodeRunner or CodePen)in your LMS to practice the following:
assignments.activity:
1. Explain the concept of polymorphism in Java and provide an example demonstrating both compile-time and runtime polymorphism.
  2. Describe method overloading in Java and discuss its benefits. Provide an example illustrating method overloading with different parameter types.
  3. Differentiate between method overriding and method overloading in Java. Provide examples to illustrate each concept and explain when each is used in practice.

assignments.name: CASE STUDY
assignments.activity:
ABC Company wants to develop an Employee Management System (EMS) to manage its employee records. The system should allow adding new employees, updating their details, and retrieving employee information.

Implement the system to have a class Employee to represent an employee with the following attributes:

id (int): Unique identifier for each employee.

name (String): Name of the employee.

department (String): Department in which the employee works.

salary (double): Salary of the employee.

The Employee class should provide encapsulation by making all attributes private.

assignments.name: PRACTICALS
assignments.intro: Demonstration of practical use of knowledge acquired
assignments.activity: Write a code snippet of a Java programming language to show implementation of polymorphism and overloading using the code runner in LMS

assignments.activity:
Overview

This case study involves designing and implementing a Library Management System using Java, focusing on various object relationships like association, aggregation, and composition. The system manages books, authors, and library members, demonstrating how these entities interact with each other.

Objectives

    To understand and implement different types of object relationships in Java.

    To design a system that showcases association, aggregation, and composition.

Key Components

    Book: Represents a book in the library.

    Author: Represents the author of a book.

    LibraryMember: Represents a member of the library.

    Library: Manages books and library members.

Object Relationships

    Association: Between LibraryMember and Book (A member can borrow books).

    Aggregation: Between Library and LibraryMember (Library has members, but members can exist without the library).

    Composition: Between Book and Author (A book cannot exist without an author).

UML Diagram

Class Diagram:

Classes and Relationships

assignments.activity:
Overview

This case study involves designing and implementing a Payment Processing System using Java, focusing on abstract classes and interfaces. The system handles multiple payment methods such as credit cards, PayPal, and bank transfers, demonstrating the use of abstract classes and interfaces to define and manage different payment types.

Objectives

    To understand and implement abstract classes and interfaces in Java.

    To design a system that utilizes abstract classes and interfaces to manage different types of payment methods.


Key Components

    Payment: An abstract class representing a general payment.

    CreditCardPayment: A class extending the Payment abstract class, representing a credit card payment.

    PayPalPayment: A class extending the Payment abstract class, representing a PayPal payment.

    BankTransferPayment: A class extending the Payment abstract class, representing a bank transfer payment.

    PaymentProcessor: An interface defining methods for processing payments.

assignments.activity:
Overview

This case study involves designing and implementing a Library Management System using Java, focusing on the use of libraries and packages. The system will manage books, members, and borrowing transactions, demonstrating how to organize code using packages and leverage external libraries for functionality like database management and user interface components.

Objectives

    To understand and implement Java packages for organizing code.

    To use external libraries for database management and graphical user interfaces.

    To develop a maintainable and scalable system using object-oriented principles.

Key Components

    Packages:

        com.library.management: Main package containing all the core classes.

        com.library.management.models: Sub-package for data models (Book, Member, Transaction).

        com.library.management.services: Sub-package for service classes (BookService, MemberService).

        com.library.management.ui: Sub-package for user interface classes.

        com.library.management.database: Sub-package for database connection and operations.

    External Libraries:

        JDBC (Java Database Connectivity) for database management.

        Swing for graphical user interface components.

assignments.activity:
Overview

In this case study, we will explore the application of error handling with exceptions in an online banking system using Java. The system will include functionalities for account management, fund transfers, and balance inquiries. Effective error handling is critical in ensuring the reliability and robustness of the system, especially in dealing with invalid operations, network issues, and database errors.

Objectives

    To understand and implement error handling using exceptions in Java.

    To ensure system robustness by catching and handling various types of exceptions.

    To improve user experience by providing meaningful error messages and ensuring smooth operation despite errors.

Key Components

    Account Management:

        Create account

        View account details

    Transactions:

        Transfer funds

        Check balance

    Error Handling:

        Handle invalid account operations

        Handle insufficient funds

        Handle network and database errors

Implementation

css

com.bankingsystem

assignments.activity:
Overview

In this case study, we will explore the application of Object-Oriented Analysis and Design (OOAD) using Java to develop a Library Management System (LMS). This system will help manage library operations, including book inventory, member records, and loan transactions. By applying OOAD principles and using UML diagrams, we will design a robust and scalable system.

Objectives

    To apply OOAD principles to analyze and design a real-world application.

    To use UML diagrams for visualizing and planning the system architecture.

    To implement the designed system using Java, demonstrating the application of object-oriented programming concepts.

Key Components


    Book Management:

        Add, update, delete, and search for books.

    Member Management:

        Register, update, delete, and search for library members.

    Loan Management:

        Issue, return, and track loaned books.

OOAD and UML Diagrams

    Use Case Diagram

        Identifies the actors (Librarian, Member) and their interactions with the system (manage books, manage members, issue books, return books).

    Class Diagram

        Represents the classes involved, their attributes, methods, and relationships.

    Sequence Diagram

        Illustrates the sequence of interactions between objects for key operations like issuing a book.

    Activity Diagram

        Describes the workflow of major processes like member registration and book issuance.

Design

Use Case Diagram

diff

Actors: 

- Librarian

- Member

Use Cases:

- Manage Books

- Manage Members

- Issue Books

- Return Books
